metadata.title: Investigating the Coverage of China’s Vaccine Crisis on YouTube: Networked Framing, Grassroots Activism, and Homophily

abstract:
This study investigates how China's domestic vaccine crisis in 2018 was politicized within Chinese video content on YouTube. The analysis, employing the Structural Topic Model, uncovered a notable hyper-politicization of the vaccine crisis in video comments, often diverging from the issue frames presented in video titles. Furthermore, inferential network analysis using the Exponential Random Graph Model revealed the hyperpoliticized comments can be attributed to 1) the emergence of networked commenters engaging in crossvideo commenting practices, and 2) political homophily among video producers, particularly regarding their critical political stance toward China. The findings suggest the networked dynamics of ssue politicization.

We examine China's domestic vaccine crisis, which occurred in 2018 prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, as a case study to investigate how its representation was politicized on YouTube within the global social media environment. Initially, we analyze the politicization of three issue frames (i.e., episodic, thematic, off-topic) presented in video titles and comments using content analysis and automated text analysis (i.e., Structural Topic Model). Subsequently, we employ inferential network analysis with ERGM to explore how features of video producers shape commenters' networked and politicized discussions. This study serves two primary purposes. Firstly, we aim to build upon previous research on issue politicization (e.g., Chinn et al., 2020) by introducing a novel perspective of networked framing (Meraz & Papacharissi, 2013). The findings illustrate how domestic social issues were transformed into hyper-politicized topics charged by geopolitical conflicts in the context of China. Secondly, our goal is to elucidate the relationship between networked activism (Tufekci, 2013) and issue politicization (Chinn et al., 2020;Fowler & Gollust, 2015). This entails elucidating how video commenters operate as networked online activists, sustaining hyperpoliticized discussions based on the homophily of video producers.

sections.header_raw: Politicization of Social Issues: The Context of China
sections.header_norm: politicization of social issues the context of china
paragraphs: Politicization is the process of infusing apparently apolitical issues with political significance (Fowler & Gollust, 2015). Previous research has explored the presence of political actors in news coverage to gauge the degree of politicization of various issues, including climate change (Chinn et al., 2020), vaccination (Fowler & Gollust, 2015), and public health crises (Hart et al., 2020). As tensions between China and the United States escalate, Western media coverage of China has at times politicized seemingly neutral or apolitical topics. For instance, U.S. media has linked Beijing's air pollution challenges with concerns regarding governmental transparency, human rights, authoritarian governance, and political reforms, thereby politicizing the issue (Duan & Takahashi, 2017;Tilt & Xiao, 2010).

The representation of China's social issues in Western media often exhibits geopoliticization, framing ostensibly apolitical issues as geopolitical ones (Meunier & Nicolaidis, 2019). Recent events, such as "the China syndrome or the Trump effect," have transformed trade negotiations, initially seen as economic matters, into a discourse of "trade warfare" (Meunier & Nicolaidis, 2019, p. 103). When reporting international news, national media frequently undergo a process of "domestication," framing the issue to align with the standpoint of the host country (Lee et al., 2011). Consequently, some degree of geopoliticization can be anticipated in foreign countries' media coverage of Chinese domestic affairs, given their distinct enduring political and cultural values (Lee et al., 2011;Thussu et al., 2017).

A potential risk of politicization is its inclination toward hyper-politicization, constructing narratives irrelevant to a given issue, diverting public attention from the issue itself, and exploiting the inherent uncertainties of the issue to challenge public consensus and trust (Bolsen & Druckman, 2015). This diversion can be measured through the degree of topic relevance, an essential criterion for assessing the quality of online deliberative discussion (Stromer-Galley, 2007). In the context of issue framing, topic relevance refers to the extent to which the discussion of various sub-topics of a given issue adheres to the main issue itself (i.e., on-topic) or shifts away from it (i.e., off-topic). On-topic frames typically take two main forms: 1) episodic frames that communicate what happened in a given issue, serving an informative function (e.g., government measures, public responses); and 2) thematic frames that interpret the broader context of a given issue (e.g., issue history, institutional roots; Iyengar & Simon, 1993). In contrast, hyperpoliticization, emphasizing that a rational discussion or debate on a given issue is overshadowed by a focus on politically charged but often irrelevant narratives (e.g., political conspiracy), is a common form of off-topic framing.

sections.header_raw: Unpacking Issue Politicization: Networked Framing, Activism, and Homophily
sections.header_norm: unpacking issue politicization networked framing activism and homophily
paragraphs: The rise of networked publics on social media has showcased the power of networked framing -a process where a given issue, often reported by media agencies, transforms into a collective politicized narrative (Meraz & Papacharissi, 2013;Varnelis, 2012). Relevant studies have addressed diverse public issues such as vaccination (Yousefinaghani et al., 2021), COVID-19related global participation (Liu et al., 2022), and the refugee crisis (Siapera et al., 2018). Networked framing involves the consistent revision, re-articulation, and redistribution of issue frames by both elite (e.g., media agencies) and non-elite actors (e.g., ordinary netizens) in a crowd-sourcing manner (Meraz & Papacharissi, 2013). This perspective is crucial for understanding the process of issue politicization as it not only focuses on framed messages but also emphasizes both semantic and relational dynamics -the networked patterns through which messages are shaped, shared, and spread (Meraz & Papacharissi, 2013). For instance, the #DoctorSpeakUp hashtag, initially created for pro-vaccination purposes, was hijacked by anti-vaccine activists to disseminate their opinions against vaccination on Twitter (Bradshaw, 2023). Thus, adopting the perspective of networked framing would assist in clarifying and interpreting issue politicization (e.g., episodic/thematic issues involving political actors) and hyper-politicization (e.g., off-topic issues with political actors), thereby advancing the evaluation method of previous research (Chinn et al., 2020).

Furthermore, politically motivated users sharing a common identity can leverage social media affordances to collectively present their political selves and draw public attention to their advocated issues -a phenomenon known as networked activism (Tufekci, 2013). While networked activism has broadened the political participation of ordinary users, it has sometimes led to excessive politicization. For example, anti-vaccine activists frequently used association affordances (e.g., hashtags, mentions) to mobilize distinct publics and propagate political conspiracies (Sharfstein et al., 2021;Yousefinaghani et al., 2021). Despite the recognized relationship between networked activism and issue politicization, little is known about how these networked activists organize to drive issue politicization. We focus on the mechanism of homophily -the tendency to communicate more with similar entities than with dissimilar entities -as it can effectively mobilize collective action (McPherson et al., 2001). Three specific mechanisms of homophily are investigated: assortative (attribute-based homophily), proximity (spatial/ temporal homophily), and relational (homophily driven by existing relationships between actors; Rivera et al., 2010).

sections.header_raw: Comment Space: Politicization of China's Vaccine Crisis on YouTube
sections.header_norm: comment space politicization of china s vaccine crisis on youtube
paragraphs: The 2018 Changchun Changsheng Biotechnology Company (CCBC) vaccine crisis involved the company's production of substandard rabies vaccines, with reported instances of vaccine production malpractice. This crisis garnered extensive attention on Chinese social media platforms, where the public expressed negative emotions and experienced a decline in confidence in vaccines manufactured in China (Zhou et al., 2019). Its repercussions were significant, impacting China's public health measures, vaccination information dissemination, and the overall well-being of children (Wang et al., 2021).

This study focuses on YouTube, a global video-sharing platform that facilitates the examination of global media and public representations of China's domestic issues. Since YouTube is not accessible in mainland China, Chinese-language video content and discussions primarily take place in regions outside mainland China (e.g., Hong Kong, Taiwan) and overseas countries with Chinese diaspora communities (e.g., United States, Australia). The predominant voices discussing China's issues on YouTube typically originate from media agencies in regions outside mainland China or overseas countries (Thussu et al., 2017). Some of these dominant media agencies exhibit animosity toward China in their coverage, including independent foreign media (Zhao, 2003) and state-controlled foreign media (Jia & Lu, 2023).

On YouTube, the core dynamic of networked framing lies in the interplay between content publishers and ordinary users (Meraz & Papacharissi, 2013). Ordinary users engage with videos by commenting to express themselves and interact with video channels and other users (Edgerly et al., 2013). User comments not only respond to video content but also re-articulate existing frames within the content. In this process, the comment space can politicize the issue, influenced by three factors. Firstly, the already politicized content generated by the video producer may trigger similarly politicized comments (Blassnig et al., 2019;Edgerly et al., 2013). Here, we highlight the video title as a key indicator and frame of video content, serving as a shorthand cue for the audience to understand how video producers label, clarify, interpret, and frame issues (De Vreese, 2005). Secondly, users can consume similar content from various video channels they subscribe to (Postigo, 2016). Repeated exposure to similar content can contribute to strengthening a user's attitude or perspective, leading them to express a politicized opinion. Additionally, ordinary users in comment spaces, as opposed to media professionals, are less constrained by professional journalistic standards, allowing them greater freedom to express politicized opinions (Blassnig et al., 2019). Therefore, we pose the following research question: RQ1: In the comment spaces discussing China's 2018 vaccine crisis, (a) how do users frame the vaccine crisis, and (b) to what extent are the issue frames of user comments influenced by the title frames of videos?

Additionally, ordinary users in comment spaces, as opposed to media professionals, are less constrained by professional journalistic standards, allowing them greater freedom to express politicized opinions (Blassnig et al., 2019). Therefore, we pose the following research question: RQ1: In the comment spaces discussing China's 2018 vaccine crisis, (a) how do users frame the vaccine crisis, and (b) to what extent are the issue frames of user comments influenced by the title frames of videos?

This crisis garnered extensive attention on Chinese social media platforms, where the public expressed negative emotions and experienced a decline in confidence in vaccines manufactured in China (Zhou et al., 2019). Its repercussions were significant, impacting China's public health measures, vaccination information dissemination, and the overall well-being of children (Wang et al., 2021).

sections.header_raw: Video's Co-commenter Network and Homophily Effect
sections.header_norm: video s co commenter network and homophily effect
paragraphs: Given that YouTube's recommendation algorithms suggest videos with similar topics to users based on their digital footprints (Kaiser & Rauchfleisch, 2020), users can view and comment across multiple videos on a given topic. This cross-video commenting connects individual comment sections, forming a networked discussion space known as the video's co-commenter network. Users who comment across multiple videos, referred to as 'cross-video commenters,' actively disseminate their opinions, potentially highly politicized, influencing the frame, tone, and atmosphere of discussions (Kramer et al., 2014). This networked discussion space can become politicized, especially if certain video channels with a critical political stance toward China produce politicized content, attracting users with similar political views and encouraging the dissemination of politicized expressions (Edgerly et al., 2013).

Three homophily mechanisms can drive the formation of edges between one video and another in the video's co-commenter network: assortative, proximity, and relational (Rivera et al., 2010). The assortative mechanism involves individuals connecting with others who share similar attributes. Media's political stance is considered a deep-rooted institutional attribute shaping its content (Schudson, 2002). Hence, two video channels with a clear and identical political stance may attract users with similar political views, leading them to comment on videos from both channels.
H1a: Videos tend to engage the same commenters when their affiliated channels share an explicitly identical political stance toward China.

The majority of grassroots media on YouTube are operated by ordinary users, and their political stance is often challenging to identify due to a lack of online public information and archives. Consequently, it remains unclear whether channels with an unknown political stance can attract the same commenters as channels with an explicit political stance (e.g., pro-China, critical of China). Therefore, we further examine the effect of attribute mixture in driving the formation of the video's co-commenter network.
RQ2: Do videos published by channels with an unknown political stance tend to engage the same commenters as videos published by channels with a pro-China or critical of China stance?

In discussions about China's domestic issues on YouTube, Chinese-language video content is mainly produced by four types of media channels: 1) statecontrolled media under the financial and/or editorial control of the state or government; 2) private-controlled media under the financial and/or editorial control of individual entrepreneurs (Schudson, 2002); 3) independent media free of influence by government and corporate (Wang, 2018); and 4) grassroots media operated by ordinary users empowered by digital technologies who contribute their opinions (Bowman & Willis, 2003). Different media types have been found to adopt different strategies in the politicization of social issues (Ylä-Anttila et al., 2019), e.g., state-controlled media may focus on promoting the government's agenda, while independent media may provide a more critical perspective. Thus, we further assume that video channels operated by the same type of media agency may share similar agendas or frames, thereby attracting commenters with similar issue interests.
H1b: Videos tend to engage the same commenters when their affiliated channels belong to the same media type.

Proximity homophily can also contribute to the formation of video's cocommenter network. Geographic proximity has been shown to contribute to social interaction networks of journalists on Twitter (Hanusch & Nölleke, 2019), as media agencies and practitioners form regional communities to target and serve local audiences (Lee et al., 2011;Vergeer, 2015). Moreover, De Francisci Morales et al. (2021) found that geographical homophily (statebased) also exists in the online interactions of Reddit users in the U.S. Thus, videos from the same geographical location may attract the same commenters.
H2: Videos tend to engage the same commenters when their affiliated channels belong to the same geographical location.

A subscription-based network of video channels is formed when channels subscribe to or are subscribed by others, influencing the formation of the video's co-commenter network. Subscription relations imply particular similarities between two channels (Liu-Thompkins, 2012) and often facilitate algorithmic recommendations of a given channel's subscribers (other channels) to ordinary users (Kaiser & Rauchfleisch, 2020), further encouraging active commenters' cross-video engagement.
H3: Videos tend to engage the same commenters when their affiliated channels share subscription relations.

sections.paper_id: Lu et al. - 2024 - Investigating the Coverage of China’s Vaccine Crisis on YouTube Networked Framing, Grassroots Activ
Lu et al. - 2024 - Investigating the Coverage of China’s Vaccine Crisis on YouTube Networked Framing, Grassroots Activ
paragraphs: We conducted a search for videos related to China's 2018 vaccine crisis on YouTube using the following Chinese keywords: "假疫苗" (fake vaccine) OR "毒疫苗" (toxic vaccine) OR "问题疫苗" (substandard vaccine) OR "疫苗事件" (vaccine incident) OR "疫苗丑闻" (vaccine scandal). Our search was limited to videos uploaded after June 15, 2018, when the vaccine crisis event was publicly reported. The search timeline concluded on December 31, 2019, as a significant subsequent developmentthe implementation of China's Vaccine Administration Lawtook effect on December 1, 2019. After removing duplicate and irrelevant videos, we identified 643 videos from 234 channels that met our criteria. Subsequently, we collected comments from each of these videos, resulting in a total of 26,852 comments from 358 videos (with 285 videos receiving no comments). Following the collection of raw data for video titles and comments from YouTube, we pre-processed the texts using the tmcn and jiebaR packages in the R language, along with manual annotation. Firstly, we transformed traditional Chinese words into simplified Chinese words using the toTrad() function in the tmcn package. Secondly, we utilized the jiebaR package to eliminate Chinese stop words embedded in the tmcn package. Thirdly, while processing title words, after the initial two steps, we further unified different title words with highly similar meanings into the same category.

sections.header_norm: human based content analysis classifying video and channel level features
paragraphs: Two trained coders, one author and one graduate research assistant with extensive research experience, conducted the coding procedure. A subsample of 15% of the collected videos was randomly selected to assess intercoder reliability on each variable described below. Once the two coders achieved a high level of agreement (i.e., Krippendorff's alpha > 0.9), they proceeded to formally code the remaining data individually.

sections.paper_id: Lu et al. - 2024 - Investigating the Coverage of China’s Vaccine Crisis on YouTube Networked Framing, Grassroots Activ
paragraphs: To identify three types of video title frames (i.e., episodic, thematic, offtopic), Stromer-Galley's (2007) classification of topic relevance was used to categorize video titles into a binary variable of issue frame: on-topic and offtopic. Following Iyengar and Simon's (1993) frame scheme, the on-topic frame was further classified into two sub-frames: episodic (communicating what happened in the issue context of China's 2018 vaccine crisis, e.g., responses of various involved companies, actions of governmental institutions, negative health influence on children and parents, policies and laws) and thematic (interpreting the broader context of China's 2018 vaccine crisis, e.g., past social scandals, reflection on the underlying causes of vaccine crisis). Moreover, linking the off-topic frame with hyper-politicization is based on previous research emphasizing that a rational discussion or debate on a scientific issue is eclipsed by a focus on politically charged (irrelevant) narratives, e.g., political conspiracy (Bolsen & Druckman, 2015). A specific classification scheme instructed coders to identify the issue frame of each video title by reviewing the keywords of each video title (see Table 1).

sections.header_raw: Structural Topic Model: Identifying Issue Frame of Comments (RQ1)
sections.header_norm: structural topic model identifying issue frame of comments rq1
paragraphs: To effectively identify the topics embedded in the vaccine-crisis discussion, we employed the Structural Topic Model (STM), a widely used method in automated text analysis (Roberts et al., 2014). The STM uses a logisticnormal distribution to analyze how the variation of topic proportions is contingent on other variables reflected by the information of metadata. Firstly, we identified the prominent topic discussed in each comment using the STM. As the searchK() function in the stm package could only identify some main discussion topics, we performed a manual inspection to determine the number of discussion topics based on diagnostic measures such as held-out likelihood (evaluation of model fit), semantic coherence (frequency of word co-occurrence within a topic), and exclusivity (uniqueness of words for each topic). This suggested several potential candidates for the K value (25,30,35,40) with relatively high values of held-out likelihood, semantic coherence, and exclusivity. Subsequently, we ran models for these K value candidates, compared the results, and ultimately selected 25 topics as the optimal K value. Additionally, we conducted a qualitative validation to assess the appropriateness of the interpretation of topic models (Chang et al., 2009). We illustrate the process of qualitative validation and manually assign a topic label for Topic 16, representing around 4.06% of all words in the dataset of video comments.

Secondly, as mentioned earlier, we utilized Stromer-Galley's (2007) classification of topic relevance and Iyengar and Simon's (1993) frame scheme to categorize the STM topics into three types of issue frame: episodic, thematic, and off-topic. A specific classification scheme guided coders to identify the issue frame of each STM topic by reviewing the top words (see Table 1).

sections.header_raw: Inferential Network Analysis: Homophily Effects on Edge Formation (H1-3 & RQ2)
sections.header_norm: inferential network analysis homophily effects on edge formation h1 3 rq2
paragraphs: A co-commenter network of videos was constructed, linking videos i and j if they shared the same commenter. To test our hypotheses and research question, we used a network-based statistical approach called Exponential Random Graph Modeling (ERGM; Lusher et al., 2013;Morris et al., 2008). ERGM generates thousands of random networks to match the observed network, allowing researchers to statistically test the likelihood of certain features in a given network. We employed ERGM to understand how the formation of the video's co-commenter network is influenced by video channel media attributes, including the type of media agency, geographical location, stance toward China, and network property represented by subscription relations among video channels. The ERGM terms used for testing are listed as follows (Morris et al., 2008): 1) nodemix (political stance) concerning the degree of coalitions between different groups as well as within-group cohesion; 2) nodematch (media type, geographical location) concerning the probability of videos co-sharing same commenters dependent on the condition of attribute match; and 3) edge covariate (subscription relation) indicating the extent to which the subscription relation can explain the formation of the video's co-commenter network. In addition, we used the nodematch term to control for video title frame and video's publication time, as videos with a similar title frame or published within the same day are likely to attract same commenters.

sections.header_raw: RQ1: Politicization of Comment-level Discussion Issues and Its Associations with Video Title Frames
sections.header_norm: rq1 politicization of comment level discussion issues and its associations with video title frames
paragraphs: Results indicated that the off-topic issues centered around politics were the most prevalent, followed by thematic issues involving politics and episodic issues (see Figure 1). Off-topic discussions often revolved around hyperpoliticized themes such as political conspiracies, attacks on political institutions or figures, negative historical narratives about political revolution and figures, and political conflicts between mainland China and Taiwan. The second category focused on thematic issues related to politics, primarily attributing blame for mainland China's past social scandals and problems to political institutions and figures. A subtopic also covered Japan's lessons regarding regulation and management in vaccine production. The third category concentrated on one episodic issue related to popular science about vaccine knowledge.

We also explored the association between the issue frames of video titles and those of comments. Descriptive findings revealed 390 videos with an episodic title frame, 150 videos with a thematic title frame, and 103 videos with an off-topic title frame. Notably, as shown in Figure 2, there was no statistically significant difference in the distribution of topics in the comment spaces among the three different video title frames for all topics. This suggests that the issue frames of video titles may not influence the topics of comments.

Concerning H1a and RQ2, Models 2-5 indicated that videos published by channels with a critical political stance toward China were more likely to attract cross-video commenters than by chance alone. Moreover, cross-video commenters tended to engage with videos from both channels with an unknown political stance and channels with a critical political stance toward China. Therefore, H1a was supported, and RQ2 was answered.

For H1b, Models 3-5 revealed that videos published by private-and statecontrolled media were more likely to attract cross-video commenters than by chance alone. However, videos published by independent and grassroots media were less likely to attract cross-video commenters than by chance alone. Therefore, H1b was partially supported.

Concerning H2, Models 4-5 showed that videos published by channels outside mainland China (e.g., Hong Kong, Taiwan) and overseas countries (e.g., the U.S.) were more likely to attract cross-video commenters than by chance alone. However, videos published by channels from mainland China were less likely to attract cross-video commenters than by chance alone. Therefore, H2 was partially supported.

Regarding H3, Model 5 showed that videos linked by subscription relations were more likely to attract cross-video active commenters than by chance alone. Therefore, H3 was supported. Additionally, the control model showed that videos published on the same day and videos with a thematic and off-topic frame were more likely to attract cross-video commenters than by chance alone; however, videos with an episodic frame were less likely to attract cross-video commenters than by chance alone.

sections.header_raw: The Prevalence of Hyper-Politicized Comments on Less Politicized Videos
sections.header_norm: the prevalence of hyper politicized comments on less politicized videos
paragraphs: The comment sections of videos exhibited a blend of off-topic and thematic issues, featuring numerous hyper-politicized discourses on political conspiracies (such as power struggles and unverified corruption allegations against retired political figures), trolling (including explicit language directed at political figures), and attacks (e.g., involving derogatory language aimed at smearing political institutions and figures). This indicates that a significant number of videos with episodic or thematic title frames sparked off-topic conversations marked by hyper-politicized expressions. Notably, grassroots' comment-level discussions often reinvented and rearticulated the title frames established by various media agencies, unveiling the fundamental process of networked framing (Meraz & Papacharissi, 2013). Contrary to prior research emphasizing the impact of video content (Edgerly et al., 2013), our findings suggest that the video title frame, as a message-level factor, may not significantly influence audience responses. Instead, a key factor may lie in the issue agenda set by foreign media agencies (video channels), especially those with a critical political stance toward China, which plays a pivotal role in mobilizing hyper-politicized responses (Jia & Lu, 2023;Wu & Li, 2017).

The majority of YouTube video titles revolved around the 2018 vaccine crisis in China, either focusing on episodic developments or interpreting the crisis through the lens of past social scandals and institutional problems (thematic issues). Nevertheless, these titles consistently included politically charged language, contributing to the politicization of the issue (Hart et al., 2020). Further details about the political relevance of video title words (politics-related vs. non-politicsrelated) can be found in Table 3. For example, a grassroots video channel (@hongkongstock) from Hong Kong released a video with an episodic title: "Police Arrests CCBC Chairman Gao Junfang in the Vaccine Incident"; a professional media channel (@Newsline_Express) operated by Hong Kong i-cable News published a video with a thematic title: "China's Vaccine Problems: Experts Say Problems Lie in Vaccine Procurement and Regulation." Some issue politicizations are relatively moderate, given the Chinese government's broad responsibility across all sectors of society (Huang, 2018). However, some video titles with an offtopic frame use politics-related words to generate hyper-politicized themes, diverting attention from the issue itself and emphasizing conspiracies and attacks against political figures and institutions. For example, a grassroots media outlet (@wenzhaoofficial) in the U.S., generated a video titled: "Is there a Power Struggle Hidden Behind the Developments of the Vaccine Case?" This nuanced classification of (neutral) politicization and hyper-politicization advances prior research on issue politicization that emphasizes the prominence of political actors in news coverage ( Chinn et al., 2020;Hart et al., 2020). The perspective of networked framing contributes to a fresh understanding of issue politicization by revealing how political actors are embedded in specific message frames with distinct contextual meaning.
sections.text:
The comment sections of videos exhibited a blend of off-topic and thematic issues, featuring numerous hyper-politicized discourses on political conspiracies (such as power struggles and unverified corruption allegations against retired political figures), trolling (including explicit language directed at political figures), and attacks (e.g., involving derogatory language aimed at smearing political institutions and figures). This indicates that a significant number of videos with episodic or thematic title frames sparked off-topic conversations marked by hyper-politicized expressions. Notably, grassroots' comment-level discussions often reinvented and rearticulated the title frames established by various media agencies, unveiling the fundamental process of networked framing (Meraz & Papacharissi, 2013). Contrary to prior research emphasizing the impact of video content (Edgerly et al., 2013), our findings suggest that the video title frame, as a message-level factor, may not significantly influence audience responses. Instead, a key factor may lie in the issue agenda set by foreign media agencies (video channels), especially those with a critical political stance toward China, which plays a pivotal role in mobilizing hyper-politicized responses (Jia & Lu, 2023;Wu & Li, 2017).

sections.paper_id: Lu et al. - 2024 - Investigating the Coverage of China’s Vaccine Crisis on YouTube Networked Framing, Grassroots Activ
sections.header_raw: Networked Hyper-Politicized Discussions and Political Homophily
sections.header_norm: networked hyper politicized discussions and political homophily
paragraphs: Approximately 23% of commenters engaged in cross-video commenting practices, expressing hyper-politicized opinions and holding influential positions within the comment networks. These active commenters play a crucial role in spreading politicized discourses and influencing the expressions of others across videos. This finding resembles Tufekci's (2013) concept of networked activism and demonstrates its impacts on issue politicization. Ordinary users were collectively mobilized through cross-video commenting in response to China's domestic vaccine crisis, which transformed isolated post-video comment spaces into a networked and hyper-politicized discussion space. This theoretical linkage extends existing literature on issue politicization (Chinn et al., 2020;Fowler & Gollust, 2015;Hart et al., 2020). ERGM-based results for the video's co-commenter network supported assortative homophily regarding a critical political stance toward China. Connected videos were predominantly produced by pan-Green media agencies from Taiwan, pro-democracy grassroots media from Hong Kong, independent media from the U.S., and state-controlled media from the U.S. For commenters expressing hyper-politicized opinions against China, their frequent interaction with video channels of critical political stance toward China indicates resonance in political alignment. Furthermore, the network exhibited edges between nodes of two political stances (critical of China and unknown), suggesting potential attribute-based similarities, such as political stance, between grassroot media with an unknown political stance and media agencies with a critical political stance toward China. This complexity in cross-cutting discussions and dissemination of hyper-politicized discourses reveals the intricate dynamics within the network. Cross-video commenters engaging two types of channels with distinct political stances could facilitate cross-cutting discussions and disseminate hyper-politicized discourses to influence grassroots media with an unknown political stance as well as their audiences.

Assortative homophily was also evident regarding specific media types (private-controlled, state-controlled) and there was proximity homophily regarding geographical location. However, based on post-hoc ERGM analysis (see Table 4), heterophily, the tendency of actors to connect with dissimilar others, was present in the network regarding media type and geographical location. Thus, media type and geographical location might not explain the formation of the video's co-commenter network from the perspective of homophily. When media agencies from regions/countries outside mainland China covered a China's domestic social issue, their audiences tended to generate hyper-politicized expressions against mainland China, contributing to certain geopolitical conflicts (Liu et al., 2022).

The relational mechanism (via subscription relation) of homophily was supported. Notably, post-hoc analysis of the channel's subscription network (see Figure 3) found that channels with an unknown political stance (52.5% of total nodes) and channels with a critical political stance toward China (40%) were dominant, central, and tightly connected in the network. This network structure indicates potential homophily (e.g., political stance) between these two types of channels discussed in our ERGM-based analysis. Subscription relations, as implicit social cues, reveal the likelihood of homophily between channels, as video channels often subscribe to any channels they are interested in (Liu-Thompkins, 2012).

In summary, a critical political stance toward China emerged as a significant factor in the formation of the video's co-commenter network. Video channels with an a critical political stance toward China, spanning various media agencies from different geographical locations, played a crucial role. The revealed political homophily provides insights into how networked activists are mobilized to drive issue politicization (Liu et al., 2022;Tufekci, 2013). The hyper-politicized discourses in comments against political figures and institutions can be attributed to a political homophily between video channels and commenters in their negative political attitudes or stances toward mainland China. This alignment may be reinforced through longterm subscription relationships, where video content consistently influences and cultivates channel followers or other interested users. When the vaccine crisis erupted as a negative social event of mainland China, those audiences were effectively mobilized by the negative issue agenda of video content and the political stance of their subscribed or interested video channels. Moreover, under the influence of video producers with a critical political stance toward China, an active group of cross-video commenters interconnected and drove the hyper-politicized expressions, thus contributing to the collective politicization of China's domestic social event.

paragraphs: The current study has several limitations. Computer-based text analysis may not achieve high accuracy, especially in identifying issue frames within short textual content. The inability to effectively identify the political stance of grassroots video channels is acknowledged, and future research will address this through an analysis of channels' past video content. Moreover, a more nuanced analysis of video content by transforming it into textual scripts is proposed for future research, as we currently rely on video titles alone for understanding content.
